chunks.text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**WRITTEN EVIDENCE SUBMITTED BY CEASE UK (OSB90)**

**ONLINE SAFETY BILL: COMMITTEE STAGE**

**Written Evidence outlining the need for amendments introducing additional regulations for online pornography**

***EXECUTIVE SUMMARY:***

The Online Safety Bill is a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity to lead the world in introducing regulation for online pornography that prevents it from driving the proliferation of illegal content, including child sexual abuse material, image-based sexual abuse material and "extreme pornography".

Legislation must also introduce minimum standards so that online pornography is subject to the same R18 certification standard issued by the video works authority as offline pornography.

It must stem the flood of imagery that represents the systemative sexual abuse and exploitation of women, children and other minority groups that actively undermines equality and normalises harmful sexual attitudes and behaviours.

***Our proposed amendments:***

We propose the Bill introduce a new Chapter specifically for online pornographic content with two principal mandates: firstly, websites must introduce systems and processes to verify the age and consent of all persons depicted in the images/ videos. Similar new rules and requirements have already been introduced by Mastercard for Adult websites \[1\].

Secondly, siites must carry out an adult risk assessment, using the BBFC’s classification for R18 offline content as the minimum standard for pornographic content, thus aligning the digital and the real world. This standard is already in place for Adult websites in Ofcom’s new Video Sharing Platform regime. \[2\]
chunks.display_text:
Section heading: Online Safety Bill

**WRITTEN EVIDENCE SUBMITTED BY CEASE UK (OSB90)**

**ONLINE SAFETY BILL: COMMITTEE STAGE**

**Written Evidence outlining the need for amendments introducing additional regulations for online pornography**

***EXECUTIVE SUMMARY:***

The Online Safety Bill is a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity to lead the world in introducing regulation for online pornography that prevents it from driving the proliferation of illegal content, including child sexual abuse material, image-based sexual abuse material and "extreme pornography".

Legislation must also introduce minimum standards so that online pornography is subject to the same R18 certification standard issued by the video works authority as offline pornography.

It must stem the flood of imagery that represents the systemative sexual abuse and exploitation of women, children and other minority groups that actively undermines equality and normalises harmful sexual attitudes and behaviours.

***Our proposed amendments:***

We propose the Bill introduce a new Chapter specifically for online pornographic content with two principal mandates: firstly, websites must introduce systems and processes to verify the age and consent of all persons depicted in the images/ videos. Similar new rules and requirements have already been introduced by Mastercard for Adult websites \[1\].

Secondly, siites must carry out an adult risk assessment, using the BBFC’s classification for R18 offline content as the minimum standard for pornographic content, thus aligning the digital and the real world. This standard is already in place for Adult websites in Ofcom’s new Video Sharing Platform regime. \[2\]

chunks.text: \[1\]. Secondly, siites must carry out an adult risk assessment, using the BBFC’s classification for R18 offline content as the minimum standard for pornographic content, thus aligning the digital and the real world. This standard is already in place for Adult websites in Ofcom’s new Video Sharing Platform regime. \[2\] NOTE: our amendments are listed here and have also been tabled as NC38, NC39 and NC40. **BACKGROUND:** ***The necessity for tighter regulation for online pornography*** Since the dawn of the internet years, pornography has moved from the margins into the mainstream. The porn industry’s sophisticated PR machine has rebranded porn as harmless recreation that embodies the values of sexual freedom, expression and empowerment. But in reality, today’s online porn is a hotbed of harmful and illegal content, serving up an infinite variety of sexual violence, misogyny and abuse. Scenarios that would have been banned, relegated to niche genres or refused classification just 20 years ago are now a part of the mainstream pornographic experience. As a society, we are finally waking up to the serious and multifaceted harms of pornography to children and it is only right that we offer them the highest levels of protection in the form of Age Verification.
chunks.display_text: NOTE: our amendments are listed here and have also been tabled as NC38, NC39 and NC40. **BACKGROUND:** ***The necessity for tighter regulation for online pornography*** Since the dawn of the internet years, pornography has moved from the margins into the mainstream. The porn industry’s sophisticated PR machine has rebranded porn as harmless recreation that embodies the values of sexual freedom, expression and empowerment. But in reality, today’s online porn is a hotbed of harmful and illegal content, serving up an infinite variety of sexual violence, misogyny and abuse. Scenarios that would have been banned, relegated to niche genres or refused classification just 20 years ago are now a part of the mainstream pornographic experience. As a society, we are finally waking up to the serious and multifaceted harms of pornography to children and it is only right that we offer them the highest levels of protection in the form of Age Verification.

chunks.text: 20 years ago are now a part of the mainstream pornographic experience. As a society, we are finally waking up to the serious and multifaceted harms of pornography to children and it is only right that we offer them the highest levels of protection in the form of Age Verification. However, this is where the regulation of pornography should begin, not where it should end. For over 20 years, the online pornography industry has been able to act with virtual impunity. Behind its defence of "free speech", it has ruthlessly pursued profit by maximising user engagement at any cost. Self-regulation has been a failure because of the strong commercial incentive for sites to maximise the amount of content they host, and to minimise moderation and safeguarding. The fierce competition in this crowded online space results in a "race to the bottom" with sites fighting to outdo one another in promoting and profiting from illegal, extreme and egregious content. This is not "just fantasy". Pornography depicts real individuals who often bear the scars, physical and psychological, of the abuse they have undergone in making it- or indeed, of being made into it against their will. Only today (22nd June 2022), the CEO and COO of Pornhub/ MindGeek resigned after a detailed expos é in the New Yorker, detailing how the world’s most popular porn site has hosted and distributed child abuse, rape and non-consensual content for years. Governments around the world need to wake to the urgent need to hold the pornography industry to account.
chunks.display_text: However, this is where the regulation of pornography should begin, not where it should end. For over 20 years, the online pornography industry has been able to act with virtual impunity. Behind its defence of "free speech", it has ruthlessly pursued profit by maximising user engagement at any cost. Self-regulation has been a failure because of the strong commercial incentive for sites to maximise the amount of content they host, and to minimise moderation and safeguarding. The fierce competition in this crowded online space results in a "race to the bottom" with sites fighting to outdo one another in promoting and profiting from illegal, extreme and egregious content. This is not "just fantasy". Pornography depicts real individuals who often bear the scars, physical and psychological, of the abuse they have undergone in making it- or indeed, of being made into it against their will. Only today (22nd June 2022), the CEO and COO of Pornhub/ MindGeek resigned after a detailed expos é in the New Yorker, detailing how the world’s most popular porn site has hosted and distributed child abuse, rape and non-consensual content for years. Governments around the world need to wake to the urgent need to hold the pornography industry to account.

chunks.text: Pornhub/ MindGeek resigned after a detailed expos é in the New Yorker, detailing how the world’s most popular porn site has hosted and distributed child abuse, rape and non-consensual content for years. Governments around the world need to wake to the urgent need to hold the pornography industry to account. In addition to the illegal content, we must be conscious of how, just as with any other form of popular media, pornography affects on us: it impacts how we see the world and in particular, how we understand sex, sexuality and gender. International academic literature from a range of disciplines, evidence from popular culture, contemporary news, and criminal law cases represent a growing body of evidence that pornography plays a pivotal and causal role in shaping real-world sexual behaviours and expectations. For this reason, the harms of pornography are felt far beyond the individual user. Although porn is consumed in private, it has profound public ramifications. Whether or not they watch it, women, children and other minority groups cannot fail to be touched by a popular genre of media grounded in sexual objectification, inequality and the abuse of power. ***The current shortcomings of the Online Safety Bill (OSB)*** The purpose of the OSB is to make the internet a safer place to be online. However, aside from preventing children’s access, the Bill does very little to regulate online pornography, despite it being at unique high risk of hosting illegal and harmful content.
chunks.display_text: In addition to the illegal content, we must be conscious of how, just as with any other form of popular media, pornography affects on us: it impacts how we see the world and in particular, how we understand sex, sexuality and gender. International academic literature from a range of disciplines, evidence from popular culture, contemporary news, and criminal law cases represent a growing body of evidence that pornography plays a pivotal and causal role in shaping real-world sexual behaviours and expectations. For this reason, the harms of pornography are felt far beyond the individual user. Although porn is consumed in private, it has profound public ramifications. Whether or not they watch it, women, children and other minority groups cannot fail to be touched by a popular genre of media grounded in sexual objectification, inequality and the abuse of power. ***The current shortcomings of the Online Safety Bill (OSB)*** The purpose of the OSB is to make the internet a safer place to be online. However, aside from preventing children’s access, the Bill does very little to regulate online pornography, despite it being at unique high risk of hosting illegal and harmful content.

chunks.text: of the Online Safety Bill (OSB)*** The purpose of the OSB is to make the internet a safer place to be online. However, aside from preventing children’s access, the Bill does very little to regulate online pornography, despite it being at unique high risk of hosting illegal and harmful content. We anticipate that only a tiny minority of the largest pornography platforms would reach the threshold of Category 1 content. These will be placed under far more regulatory duties compared with their smaller (though often higher risk) counterparts. This makes no sense, and commercially disincentives Category 1 platforms from making changes that risk putting them at a competitive disadvantage. In any case, the duties that Category 1 platforms have to fulfil are extremely ill-fitted to online pornography: some parts are relevant, some are irrelevant and some render other parts obsolete. This is perhaps an inevitable result of the Bill’s grouping together of all large user-to-user platforms, from the Guardian and LinkedIn to Pornhub and Facebook. There can be no single set of duties that is relevant and applicable to each of these. The Bill deals with categories of content based on websites’ size and functionality. In so doing, pornographic content falls between the stools.The current drafting of the Bill gives large porn sites the reason to treat the Online Safety Bill as an empty "box-ticking exercise" where they can use user empowerment or freedom of expression as justification for continuing to host harmful content.
chunks.display_text: We anticipate that only a tiny minority of the largest pornography platforms would reach the threshold of Category 1 content. These will be placed under far more regulatory duties compared with their smaller (though often higher risk) counterparts. This makes no sense, and commercially disincentives Category 1 platforms from making changes that risk putting them at a competitive disadvantage. In any case, the duties that Category 1 platforms have to fulfil are extremely ill-fitted to online pornography: some parts are relevant, some are irrelevant and some render other parts obsolete. This is perhaps an inevitable result of the Bill’s grouping together of all large user-to-user platforms, from the Guardian and LinkedIn to Pornhub and Facebook. There can be no single set of duties that is relevant and applicable to each of these. The Bill deals with categories of content based on websites’ size and functionality. In so doing, pornographic content falls between the stools.The current drafting of the Bill gives large porn sites the reason to treat the Online Safety Bill as an empty "box-ticking exercise" where they can use user empowerment or freedom of expression as justification for continuing to host harmful content.

chunks.text: functionality. In so doing, pornographic content falls between the stools.The current drafting of the Bill gives large porn sites the reason to treat the Online Safety Bill as an empty "box-ticking exercise" where they can use user empowerment or freedom of expression as justification for continuing to host harmful content. The Bill requires a specific part that deals with pornographic content, wherever it occurs on the internet, to ensure it is regulated in way proportionate to the unique harms it poses. All platforms or websites hosting such content should be under a set of specific and relevant obligations to ensure that it is legal and that it meets certain minimum standards. ***Freedom of expression v equality*** In society that champions sex equality, abhors sexual violence and recognises the harms of objectification, we fail to subject pornography to the same standards we demand in other popular forms of media. Pornography consistently normalises male sexual entitlement and the abuse of power. Sex somehow makes the violence invisible or acceptable. Our laws exist in order to protect the rights and freedoms of our most vulnerable citizens. The Equalities Act 2010 delineates the limits of freedom of expression, boundaries that are based on evidence of real-world harm rather than on abstract notions of morality or ideology. **For more detailed information:** CEASE, Expose Big Porn: Uncovering the online commercial pornography industry and the urgent need for regulation, July 2021 *June 2022* --- \[1\] AN 5196 Revised Standards for New Specialty Merchant Registration Requirements for Adult Content Merchants \[2\] https://www.ofcom.org.uk/\_\_data/assets/pdf\_file/0019/226306/vsp-industry-guide.pdf. Note, this is only for porn sites based in the UK- and it is currently only voluntary.

Pornography consistently normalises male sexual entitlement and the abuse of power. Sex somehow makes the violence invisible or acceptable.
